skill.name: task-planning
skill.description: Plan and organize software development tasks effectively using agile methodologies. Creates user stories, breaks down epics, manages backlogs, and supports sprint planning with INVEST criteria and MoSCoW prioritization.
skill.summary: Agile task planning skill for breaking down features into user stories, estimating effort, and organizing sprint backlogs.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The skill is a documentation-only file containing markdown templates and examples for agile task planning. The detected backtick patterns are markdown code block syntax (```), not shell execution. URL references are documentation links. No executable code, network calls, or system commands are present.

content.user_title: Plan and organize software development tasks
content.value_statement: Transform vague feature requests into actionable development tasks with clear acceptance criteria. Apply agile best practices like INVEST criteria and MoSCoW prioritization to deliver predictable sprint outcomes.
actual_capabilities: Generate user stories following INVEST criteria with clear acceptance criteria
Break down epics into stories and tasks with time estimates
Apply MoSCoW prioritization for feature ranking
Create sprint plans with velocity calculations and capacity planning
Define Definition of Ready and Definition of Done criteria
Structure task boards with Backlog, To Do, In Progress, Review, Done columns
limitations: Cannot directly integrate with project management tools like Jira or Trello
Estimates are guidance only and require team calibration
Does not track actual progress or update external task boards

use_cases.title: Feature Breakdown for Developers
use_cases.description: Break down a complex feature like user authentication into discrete user stories with acceptance criteria, technical notes, and time estimates for sprint assignment.

use_cases.title: Sprint Planning for Engineering Teams
use_cases.description: Plan a two-week sprint by selecting prioritized stories, calculating team capacity, and creating a committed sprint backlog with clear Definition of Done criteria.

use_cases.title: Backlog Grooming for Product Owners
use_cases.description: Refine backlog items by applying INVEST criteria, splitting oversized stories, identifying dependencies, and prioritizing using MoSCoW method.

prompt_templates.title: Create a User Story
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a user story for [feature] following INVEST criteria. Include acceptance criteria in Given-When-Then format, technical notes, and story point estimation.

prompt_templates.title: Break Down an Epic
prompt_templates.prompt: Break down the epic '[epic name]' into 5-7 user stories. For each story provide: story statement, acceptance criteria, tasks with hour estimates, dependencies, and story points.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize Features with MoSCoW
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply MoSCoW prioritization to these features: [list features]. Categorize each as Must Have, Should Have, Could Have, or Won't Have with justification. Include sprint assignment recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Create Sprint Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a sprint plan for a team of [N] developers over [duration]. Given these prioritized stories [list], calculate capacity, select stories within velocity, assign owners, and define sprint goal with Definition of Done.

output_examples.input: Create a user story for user registration
output_examples.output: A complete user story with the format: As a new visitor, I want to create an account, So that I can access personalized features. Includes four acceptance criteria in Given-When-Then format, technical notes covering API, database, and frontend requirements, a 5-point estimate, dependency on email service, and Must Have priority.

output_examples.input: Plan Sprint 10 for a 4-person team over 2 weeks
output_examples.output: A sprint plan including sprint goal, 160-hour capacity calculation, 30-point velocity target, eight selected stories with priorities and point totals, sprint backlog with ticket references, and a six-point Definition of Done checklist.

best_practices: Write user stories that are Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, and Testable
Ensure every story has clear acceptance criteria in Given-When-Then format before sprint start
Split any story estimated at 13 points or higher into smaller, deliverable units
anti_patterns: Writing vague requirements using words like improve, optimize, or enhance without measurable outcomes
Creating stories that depend on multiple other stories being completed first
Skipping estimation or assigning points without considering technical complexity and risk

faq.question: What is the INVEST criteria for user stories?
faq.answer: INVEST stands for Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, and Testable. These six principles ensure user stories are well-formed and ready for development.

faq.question: How do I estimate story points?
faq.answer: Use a Fibonacci-like sequence (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13) based on complexity, effort, and uncertainty. Calibrate as a team by comparing stories to a baseline reference story.

faq.question: What is MoSCoW prioritization?
faq.answer: MoSCoW categorizes features as Must Have, Should Have, Could Have, or Won't Have. This helps teams focus on critical functionality first and manage scope effectively.

faq.question: When should I split a user story?
faq.answer: Split stories that are 13 points or larger, span multiple acceptance criteria that could be delivered separately, or combine distinct user goals into one story.

faq.question: What is the difference between Definition of Ready and Definition of Done?
faq.answer: Definition of Ready specifies criteria a story must meet before entering a sprint. Definition of Done specifies criteria that must be met for a story to be considered complete.

faq.question: How do I handle dependencies between stories?
faq.answer: Document dependencies explicitly in each story, prioritize prerequisite stories in earlier sprints, or combine dependent stories if they are tightly coupled.
